demographics: I am 27 years old. I am female. I am White. I am married. I completed high school (12th grade). My family income is $50,000 to $59,999. I am an independent (neither Democrat nor Republican). I am liberal. I have no religious affiliation. I never attend religious services.

events.event_text: I was laid off from my first full-time job at 20, just as I was starting to build financial independence. It was a tough blow, but it motivated me to go back to school and retrain in a field with more job prospects.

When I was 21, I was caught in the middle of a painful eviction process after my landlord decided to sell the property. My roommates and I had to scramble to find a new place on short notice, and it was a stressful time that really highlighted income inequality and housing issues in our city.
[Step 3 skipped: constraint could not be satisfied — Biological incoherence: child_or_close_family_member_comes_out_as_lgbtq requires age ≥ 30, got 19]
At 23, I joined my first advocacy group protesting for affordable housing, fueled by my own eviction experience. It was both exhilarating and intimidating to stand up for what I believe in.
At 24, I decided to confront my city's council about the lack of affordable housing, organizing a small protest that gained local traction.

At 24, I met Maria, a hardworking Latina immigrant, working alongside me at the non-profit. Despite our different backgrounds, we bonded over our shared passion for social justice. She educated me about her struggles with housing and xenophobia, opening my eyes to new perspectives.

At 26, I volunteered to help at a local community center serving immigrants and refugees. The struggles they shared opened my eyes to the systemic issues they face, deepening my commitment to social justice and equity.
At 18, I was sexually harassed by my manager at my first part-time job. It was a scary and upsetting experience, but it motivated me to speak up and eventually led to him being fired.

compressed_summaries.summary: This person was laid off from their first job at 20, disrupting their financial independence plans, but used it as motivation to pursue further education. At 21, they faced a stressful eviction, raising awareness of housing issues. At 23, they joined an advocacy group protesting for affordable housing, driven by their personal experience.

This person, at 24, organized a protest against their city's lack of affordable housing, and later, at 26, volunteered at a community center serving immigrants and refugees, deepening their commitment to social justice. At 18, they experienced sexual harassment at work, which motivated them to speak up and lead to their harasser's dismissal.

persona_card: This 27-year-old, liberal, unaffiliated woman, though raised with some traditional values, is now socially progressive, advocating for affordable housing and social justice. She believes in substantial economic redistribution, recognizing structural racial inequalities, and trusts institutions cautiously. She is welcoming towards outgroups, having volunteered with immigrants and refugees, yet maintains a nuanced view of the role of government and traditional norms.
